content.body:
Sound = longitudinal mechanical wave
Needs medium to travel (NOT through vacuum)
Frequency range:
  Infrasound: <20 Hz
  Audible: 20 Hz – 20,000 Hz
  Ultrasound: >20,000 Hz

Speed of sound:
  Air (20°C): ~343 m/s
  Water: ~1480 m/s
  Steel: ~5000 m/s
Speed increases in denser/stiffer media

content.title: Sound Properties
content.body:
Pitch ∝ frequency (higher f = higher pitch)
Loudness ∝ amplitude (larger A = louder)
Timbre: quality determined by harmonics

Echoes: sound reflected from surface
Time for echo: t = 2d/v (round trip)
Reverberation: multiple reflections in room

content.title: Sound Calculations
content.body:
Distance of cliff using echo:
Echo heard after 2s, v=340m/s
d = v×t/2 = 340×2/2 = 340m

Frequency of note A = 440 Hz
Wavelength: λ=v/f=343/440≈0.78m

questions.question: Sound is a transverse wave
questions.explanation: Sound is longitudinal: particles vibrate parallel to direction of travel

questions.question: Sound cannot travel through
questions.explanation: Sound needs medium (molecules to vibrate). Cannot travel through vacuum

questions.question: Increasing frequency of sound increases
questions.explanation: Higher frequency → higher pitch (more vibrations per second → higher note)

questions.question: Speed of sound in air at 20°C is approximately
questions.explanation: Speed of sound in air ≈340-343 m/s at room temperature

questions.question: Echo heard 1.5s after shout, v=340m/s. Distance to wall
Ultrasound frequency is
questions.explanation: Ultrasound: above human hearing range, >20,000 Hz

questions.question: Sound travels faster in water than air because
options: water molecules more tightly packed, transmit vibrations faster
sound is a transverse wave in water
questions.answer: water molecules more tightly packed, transmit vibrations faster
questions.explanation: Speed of sound ∝ √(bulk modulus/density). Water's stiffness dominates → faster

questions.question: Loudness of sound depends on
questions.explanation: Loudness ∝ intensity ∝ amplitude². Bigger vibrations → louder sound

questions.question: Reverberation differs from echo because
options: reverberation is multiple overlapping reflections (in a room), echo is distinct single reflection
questions.answer: reverberation is multiple overlapping reflections (in a room), echo is distinct single reflection
questions.explanation: Echo: distinct reflected sound heard >0.1s after original. Reverberation: rapid multiple reflections merge, prolonging sound

questions.question: Frequency 500Hz, speed 340m/s. Wavelength=
What is the SI unit of force?
questions.explanation: Force is measured in Newtons (N). Named after Sir Isaac Newton. 1 N = 1 kg⋅m/s².

questions.question: State Newton's Second Law of Motion.
options: An object at rest stays at rest
Every action has an equal reaction
questions.explanation: Newton's Second Law: F = ma, where F is net force, m is mass, and a is acceleration.

questions.question: What is the speed if distance = 60 m and time = 12 s?
questions.explanation: Speed = distance ÷ time = 60 ÷ 12 = 5 m/s.

questions.question: What type of wave is light?
questions.explanation: Light is a transverse electromagnetic wave — particles oscillate perpendicular to the direction of travel.

questions.question: What is the unit of electrical resistance?
questions.explanation: Resistance is measured in Ohms (Ω), named after Georg Simon Ohm.

questions.question: Using V = IR, if I = 3A and R = 4Ω, find V.
What is the acceleration due to gravity on Earth?
questions.explanation: Gravitational acceleration on Earth's surface g ≈ 9.8 m/s² (often approximated as 10 m/s² in calculations).

questions.question: What is kinetic energy?
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy = ½mv², where m is mass and v is velocity. It is the energy possessed by a moving object.

questions.question: What happens to resistance as temperature increases (for most conductors)?
questions.explanation: For most metallic conductors, as temperature increases, atomic vibrations increase, impeding electron flow, so resistance increases.

questions.question: What is the frequency if period T = 0.5 s?
questions.explanation: Frequency f = 1/T = 1/0.5 = 2 Hz.

content.body: Ultrasound (>20,000 Hz) has many practical applications in medicine, industry and navigation. Resonance is an important phenomenon in sound.

Ultrasound applications:
Medical imaging (sonography): foetal scans, organ imaging
Echolocation: bats, sonar
Ultrasonic cleaning: high-frequency vibration removes dirt
Non-destructive testing: detect cracks in metals
Speed of sound in tissue: ~1500 m/s

Resonance:
Natural frequency: frequency at which object vibrates most easily
Resonance: driving frequency = natural frequency → large amplitude

Standing waves in pipes:
Open pipe: harmonics f=nv/2L (n=1,2,3...)
Closed pipe: f=nv/4L (n=1,3,5 odd only)

content.title: Resonance Examples
content.body:
Tacoma Narrows Bridge (1940): wind caused resonance → collapsed
Opera singer breaking glass: hits natural frequency of glass
MRI: resonance of atomic nuclei
Radio tuning: circuit resonant frequency = radio frequency

content.title: Ultrasound Timing
content.body:
Ultrasound pulse takes 0.15ms to return from foetal surface:
d=v×t/2=1500×0.00015/2=0.1125m=11.25cm

questions.question: Ultrasound medical scans use
questions.explanation: Ultrasound imaging: pulses of >20kHz sound sent into body; reflections from tissue boundaries create image

questions.question: Resonance occurs when
options: driving frequency matches natural frequency of object
questions.answer: driving frequency matches natural frequency of object
questions.explanation: Resonance: maximum energy transfer when forcing frequency = natural/resonant frequency

questions.question: Bats use echolocation to
options: navigate and hunt using ultrasonic pulses and reflected echoes
questions.answer: navigate and hunt using ultrasonic pulses and reflected echoes
questions.explanation: Bats emit >100kHz ultrasonic pulses; brain processes returning echoes to determine distance and direction of objects

questions.question: Open pipe fundamental frequency f=v/2L. L=0.5m, v=340m/s. f=
Closed pipe (closed at one end) only supports
questions.explanation: Closed end = displacement node. Only odd harmonics (n=1,3,5) fit: fundamental plus 3rd, 5th... harmonics

questions.question: Ultrasound pulse in tissue v=1500m/s, returns in 0.0001s. Depth of reflector=
Non-destructive testing with ultrasound detects
options: cracks/flaws inside metal objects without damaging them
questions.answer: cracks/flaws inside metal objects without damaging them
questions.explanation: Ultrasonic testing: sound reflects from internal boundaries. Flaw creates extra reflection → crack detected without cutting

questions.question: Tacoma Narrows Bridge collapsed due to
options: wind-driven resonance matching bridge natural frequency
questions.answer: wind-driven resonance matching bridge natural frequency
questions.explanation: Aerodynamic flutter from wind matched bridge's natural frequency → ever-growing oscillations → structural failure

questions.question: Why can't ultrasound image bones well?
options: Ultrasound mostly reflected at soft tissue-bone boundary (impedance mismatch), not transmitted
questions.answer: Ultrasound mostly reflected at soft tissue-bone boundary (impedance mismatch), not transmitted
questions.explanation: Large acoustic impedance mismatch at soft tissue/bone boundary → most ultrasound reflected, little penetrates bone

questions.question: Tuning fork resonance: when fork vibrates near same-pitch string, string vibrates because
questions.explanation: String's natural frequency matches fork frequency → resonance → string vibrates with large amplitude

questions.explanation: Force is measured in Newtons (N). Named after Sir Isaac Newton. 1 N = 1 kg⋅m/s².

options: An object at rest stays at rest

content.body:
Doppler effect: apparent change in frequency when source and observer move relative to each other

Source moving toward observer:
f_obs = f_source × v/(v-v_s)
(higher observed frequency)

Source moving away:
f_obs = f_source × v/(v+v_s)
(lower observed frequency)

v = speed of sound, v_s = speed of source

Applications: police radar, weather radar, medical Doppler ultrasound, red-shift of galaxies

content.title: Doppler Examples
content.body:
Ambulance siren 500Hz, moves at 30m/s, v=340m/s:
Approaching: f=500×340/(340-30)=500×340/310=548Hz
Receding: f=500×340/(340+30)=500×340/370=459Hz

Red-shift: galaxies moving away → lower frequency light

content.title: Applications
content.body:
Police radar: measures frequency shift of reflected radio waves → vehicle speed
Medical Doppler: measures blood flow velocity from frequency shift of reflected ultrasound
Weather radar: detects wind speeds from Doppler shift of reflected radio waves

questions.question: Doppler effect is observed when
options: source or observer moves relative to each other, changing observed frequency
questions.answer: source or observer moves relative to each other, changing observed frequency
questions.explanation: Doppler: relative motion between source and observer compresses or stretches the wave pattern → pitch change

questions.question: Ambulance approaching: observed pitch is
questions.explanation: Approaching source: wave fronts compressed → shorter wavelength → higher frequency → higher pitch

questions.question: As ambulance passes and moves away, pitch
questions.explanation: As source passes: frequency shifts from above emitted (approaching) to below emitted (receding) — characteristic pitch drop

questions.question: Doppler formula: f_obs = f_s×v/(v-v_s). Source speed 0 gives
questions.explanation: If v_s=0: f_obs=f_s×v/v=f_s (no Doppler shift when stationary)

questions.question: Police speed radar uses Doppler by
options: measuring frequency shift of reflected radio waves from moving vehicle
questions.answer: measuring frequency shift of reflected radio waves from moving vehicle
questions.explanation: Radar gun: transmits radio waves, measures Doppler shift of echo from moving car → calculates speed

questions.question: Redshift of galaxies is analogous to Doppler because
options: light from receding galaxies has longer wavelength (lower frequency) — same principle as receding sound
questions.answer: light from receding galaxies has longer wavelength (lower frequency) — same principle as receding sound
questions.explanation: Cosmological redshift: as galaxy moves away, light waves stretched → longer λ, lower f → shifted toward red end

questions.question: Source f=400Hz, v_sound=340m/s, v_source=40m/s approaching. Observed f=
Medical Doppler ultrasound measures
options: frequency shift of reflected ultrasound from moving blood cells → blood flow velocity
questions.answer: frequency shift of reflected ultrasound from moving blood cells → blood flow velocity
questions.explanation: Doppler ultrasound: detects shift in frequency of ultrasound reflected from moving red blood cells → flow velocity

questions.question: Bat detecting a stationary moth uses which effect?
questions.explanation: If bat moves toward stationary moth, reflected echolocation pulses are Doppler-shifted → bat detects closing speed

questions.question: Source behind observer, moving away at speed v_s. f_obs =
questions.explanation: Receding source: f_obs=f_s×v/(v+v_s). Denominator larger → f_obs smaller → lower pitch

content.body:
Harmonics and overtones:
Fundamental (1st harmonic): simplest standing wave
2nd harmonic = 1st overtone: twice fundamental
3rd harmonic etc.

String instrument: both ends fixed
Harmonics: f_n = nv/2L (n=1,2,3...)
Wave speed on string: v=√(T/μ) (T=tension, μ=mass/length)

Wind instruments (open pipe):
f_n = nv/2L
Flute, oboe (effectively open)

Closed pipe (one end closed):
f_n = nv/4L  (n=1,3,5 odd)

content.title: String Examples
content.body:
Guitar string L=0.65m, v=400m/s
Fundamental: f=v/2L=400/1.3=308Hz
2nd harmonic: f=2×308=615Hz
3rd harmonic: 3×308=923Hz

Tuning: higher tension T → higher v → higher pitch
Shorter string → higher pitch

content.title: Recorder (closed pipe)
content.body:
L=0.30m, v=340m/s
Fundamental: f=340/(4×0.30)=283Hz
3rd harmonic: f=3×283=850Hz
(no 2nd harmonic — closed pipe)

questions.question: Fundamental frequency is
questions.explanation: Fundamental (1st harmonic): simplest resonant frequency of string/pipe

questions.question: String both ends fixed, L=1m, v=400m/s. Fundamental f=
2nd harmonic of a string is
questions.explanation: 2nd harmonic (1st overtone) = twice the fundamental frequency

questions.question: Closed pipe (one end closed) plays
questions.explanation: Closed pipe: displacement node at closed end → only odd harmonics (1st, 3rd, 5th)

questions.question: To raise pitch of guitar string you should
questions.explanation: v=√(T/μ): higher tension T → higher wave speed → higher frequency/pitch

questions.question: Flute (open pipe) L=0.66m, v=340m/s. Fundamental =
Shortening a string while playing (fretting) does what?
questions.explanation: Shorter string L → higher fundamental f=v/2L → higher pitch (what guitarists do when fretting)

questions.question: Timbre (tone quality) of instruments differs due to
questions.explanation: Same note on violin vs piano: same fundamental but different harmonic content → different sound quality

questions.question: Resonance box of violin amplifies sound by
options: creating sympathetic vibration at same frequency as string
questions.answer: creating sympathetic vibration at same frequency as string
questions.explanation: Resonance cavity: shaped to resonate at string frequencies → couples string vibration efficiently to air → louder sound

questions.question: Open organ pipe 3rd harmonic vs closed pipe 3rd harmonic frequency ratio
questions.explanation: Open: f_3=3v/2L. Closed: f_3=3v/4L. Ratio=(3v/2L)/(3v/4L)=2. So open 3rd=2×closed 3rd. Ratio 2:1 (open higher)

content.body: In this lesson, we explore advanced aspects of Sound. Build on what you have already learned and apply your knowledge to more challenging problems.
Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson you should be able to: (1) Apply Sound concepts to complex problems. (2) Use appropriate formulas and methods. (3) Interpret results in context. (4) Connect this topic to related areas of physics.
Core Theory

This section of Sound builds on foundational knowledge. In physics, advanced problem-solving requires not just knowing formulas but understanding when and how to apply them. Study each worked example carefully.

content.title: Worked Example 5.1
content.body: Consider a more complex problem on Sound. Approach: (1) Read and understand the problem. (2) Identify all given information. (3) Determine which concepts apply. (4) Solve systematically, showing all steps. (5) Check your answer is reasonable.

content.title: Worked Example 5.2
content.body: Another type of problem you may encounter: Questions may test your ability to combine multiple concepts within Sound. Practice identifying the type of problem and the appropriate method before attempting to solve.
Common examination questions on Sound include: definitions and explanations, numerical calculations, diagram interpretation, and analysis of given data. Make sure you can handle all types.
Summary and Review
You have now covered all lessons in Sound. Review the key concepts from each lesson, practice past paper questions, and ensure you can apply your knowledge confidently. Good luck in your studies!

questions.question: What is the SI unit of force?
questions.explanation: Force is measured in Newtons (N). Named after Sir Isaac Newton. 1 N = 1 kg⋅m/s².

questions.question: State Newton's Second Law of Motion.
options: An object at rest stays at rest
Every action has an equal reaction
questions.explanation: Newton's Second Law: F = ma, where F is net force, m is mass, and a is acceleration.

questions.question: What is the speed if distance = 60 m and time = 12 s?
questions.explanation: Speed = distance ÷ time = 60 ÷ 12 = 5 m/s.

questions.question: What type of wave is light?
questions.explanation: Light is a transverse electromagnetic wave — particles oscillate perpendicular to the direction of travel.

questions.question: What is the unit of electrical resistance?
questions.explanation: Resistance is measured in Ohms (Ω), named after Georg Simon Ohm.

questions.question: Using V = IR, if I = 3A and R = 4Ω, find V.
What is the acceleration due to gravity on Earth?
questions.explanation: Gravitational acceleration on Earth's surface g ≈ 9.8 m/s² (often approximated as 10 m/s² in calculations).

questions.question: What is kinetic energy?
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy = ½mv², where m is mass and v is velocity. It is the energy possessed by a moving object.

questions.question: What happens to resistance as temperature increases (for most conductors)?
questions.explanation: For most metallic conductors, as temperature increases, atomic vibrations increase, impeding electron flow, so resistance increases.

questions.question: What is the frequency if period T = 0.5 s?
questions.explanation: Frequency f = 1/T = 1/0.5 = 2 Hz.

questions.question: Which quantity is a vector?
questions.explanation: Velocity is a vector quantity — it has both magnitude and direction. Speed is a scalar (magnitude only).

questions.question: What is the principle of conservation of energy?
options: Energy cannot be created or destroyed, only transformed
Energy is always lost as heat
questions.answer: Energy cannot be created or destroyed, only transformed
questions.explanation: The law of conservation of energy states that the total energy in a closed system remains constant.

questions.question: What is the unit of power?
questions.explanation: Power is measured in Watts (W). 1 W = 1 J/s. Power is the rate of energy transfer.

questions.question: What is the boiling point of water at standard pressure?
questions.explanation: Water boils at 100°C (373 K) at standard atmospheric pressure (101.325 kPa).

questions.question: In a series circuit, the current:
options: Is the same through all components
Is largest at the first component
Is zero if one component fails
questions.answer: Is the same through all components
questions.explanation: In a series circuit, there is only one path for current, so the same current flows through all components.

questions.question: What is pressure?
questions.explanation: Pressure P = F/A, where F is force applied perpendicular to a surface area A. SI unit is Pascal (Pa).

questions.question: Which type of radiation has the highest penetrating power?
questions.explanation: Gamma rays are electromagnetic radiation with very short wavelength and highest penetrating power, requiring thick lead or concrete to stop.

questions.question: What happens to light when it travels from air into glass?
questions.explanation: Light slows down when entering a denser medium (glass) and changes direction — this is refraction.

questions.question: State the law of conservation of momentum.
options: Total momentum before = total momentum after in a closed system
questions.answer: Total momentum before = total momentum after in a closed system
questions.explanation: In a closed system with no external forces, the total momentum before a collision equals the total momentum after.

questions.question: What is the half-life of a radioactive substance?
options: The time for half the radioactive nuclei to decay
The time for all nuclei to decay
The time to double in activity
questions.answer: The time for half the radioactive nuclei to decay
questions.explanation: Half-life is the time taken for the activity (or number of radioactive nuclei) to decrease to half its original value.
